Title: v0.2.5 — Multi-project + performance
Summary: One Bleepforge install now holds many projects with notebook / sync / import-once creation flows, an active-project switcher in the sidebar, and the portable content:// URL scheme so notebook projects move cleanly across machines. Switching is hot-reloaded in-process (~15ms, no app restart). Under the hood: per-domain singleton stores replace the catalog's all-fetch every mount (10 parallel /api/<domain> calls down to 0 per save in the same domain, 0 per save in unrelated domains), and image URLs gained mtime-keyed cache busters so the asset router can ship 30-day immutable headers instead of no-cache every paint.
Body:
## Headlines

### Multi-project layer — projects.json + active-project.json + /projects page

A single Bleepforge install can hold any number of projects, exactly one active at a time. The active-project chip in the sidebar (under the BLEEPFORGE wordmark) always tells you which project's data the rest of the app is showing; click it to open [Projects](/projects) and switch.

Projects live at `<bleepforgeRoot>/projects/<slug>/` with two top-level subdirs:

- `data/` — Bleepforge's JSON cache + Bleepforge-only authored content (concept, codex, dialog layouts, shader metadata).
- `content/` — notebook-mode assets + shaders. Sync mode skips this — its content lives in the Godot tree.

`bleepforgeRoot` = `dialoguer/` in dev, `~/.config/Bleepforge/` (Linux) etc when packaged. The registry (`projects.json`) and active pointer (`active-project.json`) sit at that root.

### Three creation modes

**Notebook** — standalone. Bleepforge owns everything inside the project. No Godot connection.

**Sync to Godot** — current behavior, now per-project. Pick a Godot folder; Bleepforge mirrors its `.tres` files as JSON on every boot, watches for live changes, and writes edits back on save.

**Import once from Godot** — snapshot a Godot project into a fresh notebook. `.tres` data lands as JSON, referenced images + shaders get copied into `content/`, refs get rewritten to the portable `content://` scheme. After the snapshot completes the project is fully independent — edits never touch the source Godot tree. Use for safe forking or archival.

Import-once is a *creation flow*, not a stored mode. The resulting project's `mode` field is `notebook`; the import work just happens once on the first boot after creation, gated by a manifest written into the new project's `data/`.

### content:// — portable asset references

Notebook-mode projects store image refs as `content://images/foo.png` in JSON, resolved server-side to `<contentRoot>/images/foo.png` at request time. Moving the Bleepforge install to another machine keeps the refs working (as long as the project's `content/` dir comes with it). New notebook projects start portable from day one; the import-once flow rewrites references during the seed.

Sync mode keeps absolute paths for now. Migrating existing FoB data to `content://` is wider work than v0.2.5 fit; the asset router transparently accepts both forms.

### Rename + delete

Each project card has a `⋯` menu (and right-click) with **Rename** and **Delete**. Slug stays immutable because it's the on-disk dir name + active-pointer key; only displayName changes on rename. Delete offers **Forget** (registry only — files stay at `projects/<slug>/`) or **Delete files** (also `rm -rf`s the dir). For sync-mode projects the Godot tree itself is never touched regardless of choice — Bleepforge only deletes what it owns.

You can't delete the active project (switch first) or the last project (create a new one first). Both refusals return 409 with clear messages.

### Switching is hot-reloaded in-process — no app restart

The active project's paths (data root, content root, Godot root, mode) hot-reload in place on switch. `POST /api/projects/reload` calls `reloadServerState()`: stops the watcher, re-reads the active project, mutates `config.*` + `folderAbs.*` in place, then runs the per-active-project boot sequence (project index + reconcile if sync mode, asset + shader caches, watcher rebind, pending-import seed if any). ~15ms on FoB. Works identically in dev (`pnpm dev:desktop`) and packaged AppImage — no Electron / tsx-watch lifecycle dependency.

Mechanically this required every reload-relevant path to resolve through `config.X` / `folderAbs.X` fresh on each call rather than being captured into a module-local `const` at load time. Storage modules (balloon, dialog, help, codex) now use a `const root = (): string => folderAbs.X` getter pattern. `makeJsonStorage` accepts `FolderSource = string | (() => string)` so storage instances created once at server boot survive reloads — the closure resolves the folder fresh on every list/read/write/delete.

The Electron app-restart IPC stays in place as a recovery action (Bleepforge upgrade, stuck state), but is no longer the routine swap path. The sidebar's restart icon glows when an unflushed pointer change is detected; clicking it fires the hot-reload.

### Mode-aware boot gates

Three independent gates split the boot work cleanly:

- **Sync-mode + Godot root** → ProjectIndex build + boot reconcile + .tres watcher branch.
- **contentRoot present** (either mode) → asset cache + shader cache + the content-tree watcher.
- **Notebook mode** → skips ProjectIndex + reconcile entirely. The watcher attaches to the project's `content/` instead of a Godot tree.

.tres writeback in every per-domain writer (`writeItemTres` / `writeKarmaTres` / etc) gates on `isSyncMode()`. New writers auto-inherit the gate.

### Legacy data/ → projects/<slug>/data/ migration

First boot of v0.2.5 against a v0.2.4-or-earlier install:

1. Slug derived from `concept.json`'s Title (fallback `flock-of-bleeps`).
2. `data/` contents `mv`'d into `projects/<slug>/data/`.
3. `godotProjectRoot` pulled out of `preferences.json` into the new project record.
4. Registry + active pointer written.
5. Legacy `data/` left empty as a verification window — log line prompts manual cleanup on subsequent boots.

For cross-machine clones (someone clones the repo after the migration was committed), a bootstrap path discovers existing `projects/<slug>/data/` dirs and registers them — godot root defaults to null, user fills in via Preferences if needed.

## Performance work

### Per-domain singleton stores

Pre-v0.2.5, every `useCatalog()` mount fired 10 parallel `/api/<domain>` fetches via `Promise.all`. Every list-page mount, every edit-page mount, every component that needed catalog autocomplete. After a save, the catalog refreshed in full — 10 more fetches.

Replaced by per-domain singleton stores at [client/src/lib/stores/](client/src/lib/stores/). Each domain (items / npcs / quests / karma / factions / pickups / shaders / dialogs / balloons / codex) has a module-level store built via `createStore` (flat) or `createFolderedStore` (per-folder). Stores hold `{ data, status, error }` and expose `getSnapshot / subscribe / refresh / patch / remove / ensureLoaded`. Multiple `useStore(store)` mounts share the same data, the same in-flight fetch promise, and the same SSE-driven refresh signal. React integration via `useSyncExternalStore` — no state library.

Three consumer migrations followed:

- **List pages** (v0.2.5 phase 2a) consume per-domain hooks directly instead of running their own `useEffect + api.list()`.
- **Edit pages** (v0.2.5 phase 2b) derive a single entity via `list.find(matchFn)` and seed local form state once on first store-ready.
- **Save responses patch stores directly** (v0.2.5 phase 3). Every `Api.save / remove` call now patches the relevant store from the response instead of triggering a blanket catalog refresh. SSE events route per-domain via a `SyncDomain → store` map at [wireToBus.ts](client/src/lib/stores/wireToBus.ts).

Net result: a save in one domain triggers 0 fetches for that domain (patch from response) and 0 for any other (no blanket refresh). Pre-v0.2.5, the same save fired 10 fetches across all domains.

### mtime-keyed asset cache busters

Image URLs (`assetUrl(path)`) now carry `?v=<mtime>` for files known to the client's mtime cache. The asset router ships `Cache-Control: public, max-age=2592000, immutable` for URLs with `v`, and `no-cache, must-revalidate` for URLs without. The mtime cache ([client/src/lib/assets/mtimeCache.ts](client/src/lib/assets/mtimeCache.ts)) is a module-level `Map<absPath, mtime>` seeded by one `/api/assets/images` fetch at boot and kept fresh by `Bleepforge:asset` SSE events.

Net result: the dialog graph used to re-fetch every NPC portrait + line portrait on every navigation. With the cache busters, browser HTTP caching kicks in for 30 days; portraits re-fetch only when the file actually changes (mtime flips → URL flips). On localhost this saved 5-10ms × ~15 portraits per graph mount — the dominant remaining cost of the dialog page's mount.

### Other cleanups in the cycle

- Dialog graph derives `seqs` from the store directly (no separate fetch); layouts cached per-folder in memory so revisits land instantly.
- ShaderList keeps its own `usageCounts` fetch (a separate endpoint, not part of the catalog).
- Codex meta + category-level ops fall back to `codexStore.refresh()` for the rare ops that don't have patchable response shapes.

## API additions

```
GET    /api/projects                    list + activeSlug + runtimeActiveSlug
POST   /api/projects                    create — body { displayName, mode,
                                                godotProjectRoot?, setActive? }
POST   /api/projects/import-once        seed from Godot tree — body
                                          { displayName, sourceGodotRoot }
POST   /api/projects/reload             hot-reload active project in-process
PATCH  /api/projects/:slug              rename displayName
DELETE /api/projects/:slug[?wipe=true]  forget (or rm -rf with wipe)
PUT    /api/projects/active             switch active slug — body { slug }
```

All switches + creations that flip the active pointer return `restartRequired: true` to signal

. PUT `/active` also returns `noop: true` when the requested slug already matches the running active project, so the client can skip the reload entirely. GET returns BOTH `activeSlug` (disk pointer = next-reload target) and `runtimeActiveSlug` (= what this server is currently serving) so callers can surface the brief desync window between an action and the auto-reload that follows.

## Under the hood

- New server module: [lib/projects/](server/src/lib/projects/) — registry I/O, migration, bootstrap, router, importOnce.
- New shared schema: [project.ts](shared/src/project.ts) — `Project`, `ProjectRegistry`, `ActiveProjectPointer`, `slugify`.
- New client surface: [features/projects/](client/src/features/projects/) — page, cards, chip, new-project modal.
- [config.ts](server/src/config.ts) rewired to derive `dataRoot` + `contentRoot` + `godotProjectRoot` + `projectMode` from the active project record. New helper `isSyncMode()` is the single gate point.
- [scripts/sync-userdata.mjs](scripts/sync-userdata.mjs) updated to walk per-project paths instead of the legacy single `data/` dir.

## Known issues / deferred

- **Notebook projects' Help library is per-project, not app-level**. The help dir gets copied into each project's `data/help/` instead of sitting at the Bleepforge root. Mostly harmless today (help is read-only at runtime) but a notebook fork from FoB would get a copy that diverges from the canonical seed. Likely-fix: hoist help out of per-project storage so the seed manifest tracks one location.
- **Sync-mode JSON still stores absolute paths** for image refs. The asset router accepts both `content://` and absolute paths transparently, but existing FoB data won't move until a migration pass is run. Probably v0.2.6+.
- **No "undo" on switch / delete**. Switching is reversible (switch back), deleting is reversible only if you chose Forget. Both have explicit confirms; phase 8 didn't add a full Trash surface.
- **Watcher's log prefix still says `[tres-watcher]`** even in notebook mode where there are no .tres files. Cosmetic; the watcher does watch the content tree correctly in both modes.
- **Genericize for any Godot project** — the long-term anchor. Per-project schemas + dynamic edit forms are the next big lift, on top of the multi-project foundation this release lands.

> tip: see [Projects — the multi-project layer](/help/getting-started/projects) for the user-facing walkthrough of the new system.
